video_title: Turning Point: The Vietnam War | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I just finished this series on Netflix and was impressed at how balanced the narrative was. I studied the Vietnam War/American War (depending on what side of the conflict you were on) in college as part of a class on Vietnamese history. Back then we studied it from all perspectives: U.S., South Vietnamese, National Liberation Front, North Vietnamese, Chinese, Russian, Buddhist, Catholic, and even the French and Portuguese when you take the conflict back to its origins and how colonialism affected the country. This documentary does a really good job of that same type of 360° view from each perspective and doesn’t sugar coat the U.S. government’s role in lying to the American public, using propaganda, and then trying to cover it all up. It’s critical of all administrations involved, from Truman to Nixon. It’s particularly critical of the Kennedy and Johnson administrations as well as Nixon. It does a good job of showing you different perspectives from the military, civilians, peace activists, etc. from all countries involved. The testimonials are profound. I found it to be very neutral and one of the most factual documentaries without a real bias.

video_title: Turning Point: The Vietnam War | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I’ve seen a lot of Vietnamese comments supporting the North as if the Vietnam War was simply the U.S. versus Vietnam. That’s a misunderstanding.

In reality, it was a civil war between North and South Vietnam, with the U.S. supporting the South in opposition to the North's communist regime. Many Vietnamese people supported the U.S. effort and opposed communism — and that divide still exists today. Even now, there are Vietnamese communities, especially among those who fled after the war, who remain strongly against the communist government of the North.

comment_text:
Young American men—still in their teens—were sent thousands of miles from home to defend freedom in a foreign conflict few fully understood. They answered the call of duty not for glory, but for the ideals this nation stands for. I hope Netflix honors the courage and sacrifice of these brave soldiers—men who returned home to a country that too often failed to show them the respect and gratitude they had earned.

Many forget that this war came in the shadow of Korea and World War II, at a time when the United States had only recently emerged as a global superpower. These young men carried the weight of that responsibility on their shoulders. God bless our American heroes—then, now, and always.

video_title: Turning Point: The Vietnam War | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
You're framing history through a lens that conveniently omits colonial context, geopolitical dynamics, and national self-determination. The so-called "Republic of Vietnam" was not a product of indigenous consensus, but a Cold War construct propped up by foreign powers, especially the U.S., following the French colonial retreat. The Geneva Accords of 1954 stipulated nationwide elections in 1956 to reunify the country, elections which the South, under U.S. influence, outright refused. So let’s be honest: who violated the Accords first? 
You claim the North 'invaded' the South, yet ignore that the South was never a fully sovereign state, and that resistance against the Southern regime came not only from the North, but from within. From the South’s own rural population, oppressed by a government known for political repression and corruption. The North didn’t 'invade' some independent, sovereign nation, it responded to a Western-imposed, artificial division and a puppet regime that had no popular mandate. The term "puppet army" isn't just rhetoric, the Southern government depended heavily on U.S. aid, military, and advisors from day one.
As for civilian deaths, both sides bear responsibility, but the most intense bombing campaign in human history was not launched by Hanoi, it was by Washington. Millions died because Vietnam became a battleground for a proxy war that the U.S. escalated to avoid the spread of communism, not to promote Vietnamese democracy. Framing this as North Vietnam versus America ignores the key point: IT WAS VIETNAM RESISTING FOREIGN DOMINATION. Millions of Vietnamese from both regions supported reunification and opposed the U.S. occupation. The U.S sent over half a million troops, dropped more bombs than in WWII, and committed atrocities like My Lai. All to prop up a government that couldn't survive on its own.
If you argue that reunifying the country, even through war was unjustified, then what do you say about centuries of colonial resistance? By your logic, no colonized country has the right to expel foreign-backed regimes. This was not a war against the South, it was a war for Vietnam’s sovereignty. And let’s be clear, Vietnamese didn’t flee because of 'freedom.' Many feared retribution after collaborating with a foreign occupier, or were caught in the chaos the U.S. left behind. The tragedy of displacement is real, but it doesn’t rewrite the cause of the war. There is no forgiveness for those who betray the Fatherland. This was not America vs communism. It was Vietnam vs. imperialism. Comparing Vietnam to Korea ignores the critical fact that Vietnam had a unifying nationalist movement long before foreign powers turned it into a Cold War front line. The conflict between North and South Korea also reflects a struggle against the influence of external powers. The United States seeks to prevent the spread of communism, as this could undermine its strategic influence and economic interests, including its defense industry.
Your narrative is selective, revisionist, and fundamentally dishonest if you pretend the South represented the will of the Vietnamese people while the North was merely an aggressor. 
If you seek the truth of history, come to Vietnam and learn it from the land where it happened. Do not let yourself be deceived by American narratives or by those who betrayed their own nation. The so-called Republic of Vietnam was nothing more than a puppet regime, built on the blood of traitors to the Fatherland and loyalty to foreign powers. Loser means Loser — no need for excuses — no need for excuses — no need for excuses. If you have something to say, come to Vietnam, speak Vietnamese. Don’t try to act superior with English. 
We won and with victory comes the right to judge. The defeated have no grounds to justify their failure, no matter how long they try, as America continues to do even after 50 years.  It’s time to evolve.

video_title: Turning Point: The Vietnam War | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
"Turning Point: The Vietnam War" Là Một Bộ Phim Bôi Nhọ, Xuyên Tạc Lịch Sử.

Sau 50 năm kết thúc chiến tranh thì mình đã mong việc Mỹ chấp nhận lịch sử và tiến đến tương lai như người Việt chúng ta đã làm. Nhưng chỉ sau 5 phút chiếu phim thì tất cả mọi thứ đã khiến mình rất sôi máu.

Đây không phải bộ phim duy nhất mà họ bôi nhọ ta như này. Mà họ luôn vờ như rằng mình là nạn nhân của chiến tranh và họ không muốn, họ buộc phải chiến đấu vì điều đó thông qua những bộ phim Hollywood.

Ngay từ cái tên "The Vietnam War" đã hoàn toàn là sai. Cái tên đúng sẽ luôn phải là "The Resistance War Against America To Save The Nation"

Sau đây sẽ là một số trích đoạn ở tập 1 mà mình gọi là bôi nhọ và xuyên tạc:
1. "Bắc Việt Phá Hoại các làng miền Nam và để hướng đến thống nhất cả nước. Còn miền Nam, họ chỉ muốn được đơn độc"
2. "NĐD được ví như một vị thánh sống. Mặc dù ông là một người công giáo nhưng đã góp phần xây dựng các đền chùa cho người dân của ông"
3. "Đối với tôi, người VN và Mỹ có những mâu thuẫn sâu sắc, đó là một phần lí do dẫn đến chiến tranh tại VN"- Đạo diễn phim the sympathizer(phim nâng bi 3 que)
4. Phim nói về việc phi công Mỹ ở nhà tù Hòa Lò phải chịu đựng.
(Đây chỉ là góc nhìn kể chuyện của một nhóm nhỏ “người ném bom bị bắt” mà quên đi “người dân dưới bom đã chết”. Trên thực tế nhiều tù binh Mỹ còn ví Hòa Lò như resort) 
5. Những khó khăn, đau khổ từ những người lính các đời Tống Thông Mỹ khi viện trợ cho miền nam VN. 
(Mỹ không phải nạn nhân – họ là bên chủ động can thiệp quân sự vào một quốc gia có chủ quyền đang đấu tranh thống nhất.)
6. Sự lo lắng của JFK của chủ nghĩa cộng sản hướng ra toàn thế giới thông qua VN và hướng đến ĐNÁ nên phải dẫn đến chiến 
(Không thể nhân danh “sợ cộng sản” để biến một cuộc chiến tranh giải phóng dân tộc thành một “chiến trường ủy nhiệm”. Đó là sự phi lý về đạo đức và luật pháp quốc tế.)
7. Phật giáo dường như trở thành kẻ thù trong đất nước.
(Chế độ đàn áp Phật giáo thì không thể gọi là "nạn nhân của Phật giáo". Biểu tình và tự thiêu không xảy ra nếu không có bất công và đàn áp tôn giáo.)
8. Ngày 2/8/1964  USS Maddox thu thập thông tin tình báo thì bị 3 tàu Tàu phóng lôi VN tấn công trước. 
(Tướng Phùng Thế Tài sau đó tuyên bố rằng Maddox đã bị theo dõi từ ngày 31 tháng Bảy và nó đã tấn công các tàu đánh cá vào ngày 2 tháng Tám buộc Hải quân Nhân dân Việt Nam phải đánh trả.)

Tổng kết: Đây là một bộ phim nếu nó chọn lọc dữ kiện có lợi cho Mỹ, thiếu dẫn chứng cụ thể, và làm mờ đi bản chất xâm lược của cuộc chiến. Còn việc của chúng ta là phải bảo vệ sự thật lịch sử và danh dự dân tộc Việt Nam.

🇻🇳🇻🇳🇻🇳

comment_text: I am a descendant of the Republic of Vietnam. I now live overseas, far from the communist country. I deeply thank the U.S.-led allied forces who fought alongside the Republic of Vietnam to defend our territory and freedom against the invading Viet Cong. I also express my condolences for the fallen soldiers. There are many Viet Cong supporters in this comment section, but the Vietnam War was, in part, a proxy war between the U.S. and the Soviet Union. The U.S. withdrew from the Republic of Vietnam under the 1973 Paris Peace Accords, and in 1975, the Republic of Vietnam surrendered. However, the Viet Cong could not have won the war without massive support from the Soviet Union and China. Even after the U.S. withdrawal, the Soviet Union and China continued to support North Vietnam. If the U.S. had not withdrawn under the Paris Accords, the Republic of Vietnam might still exist today.

video_title: Turning Point: The Vietnam War | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I don’t know what this documentary series will discuss, but as a Vietnamese, I truly hope it will tell the truth for those who genuinely want to understand what happened during that time — the reasons, the timeline, the goals, and the outcomes.

There are many questions I haven’t been able to answer for myself. That part of history wasn’t something I grew up thinking about, and it was never my focus in everyday life.

As a kid, I wasn’t interested in my country’s history because I found it hard to follow — too many gaps between the beginning and the end, too many political layers, and a lack of engaging storytelling.

I always wondered: How did we win? What exactly saved us? Who were our real enemies? And why does the narrative always focus so heavily on America while rarely mentioning France or China?

I’m not here to point fingers — I just want a complete and honest story. One that goes beyond the simplified “we defeated the Americans” narrative.

In this moment, what I truly respect is the truth, not who wins or who loses.

video_title: Turning Point: The Vietnam War | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Let’s set the record straight:
What the U.S. calls the "Vietnam War" is known in Vietnam as "The Resistance War Against America for National Salvation." This was not a civil war. It was a people's war for independence against foreign imperialist invasion, led by the U.S. and backed by its puppet regime in the South.

The term “Vietnam War” is a convenient name used in Western discourse to downplay America’s responsibility, masking an aggressive war of occupation as if it were just an “internal conflict.” But more than 3 million Vietnamese people died, with millions more affected by bombings, massacres, and Agent Orange, all carried out by the U.S. military on Vietnamese soil — not American soil.

Some argue that “Vietnam War” is more commonly used. Sure — but popular doesn’t mean accurate, especially when that popularity comes from the very empire that caused the war and now wants to rewrite history. You don't get to commit war crimes and then dictate how the victims should name their resistance.

Vietnam has every right to define that war based on its experience, its pain, and its struggle for liberation. This was not an internal affair — it was a war of national defense.

Also, let’s not forget: even after the U.S. lost and withdrew, they had the audacity to demand "war debt" compensation from Vietnam, a country they invaded, bombed, and poisoned.

And now some people still dare to call that a “civil war”? That’s not just wrong — it’s offensive.

comment_text:
"VietNam War" là Mỹ đang lấp liếm rằng đây chỉ là một cuộc nội chiến của Việt Nam và thất bại của Việt Nam Cộng Hoà không liên quan gì tới Mỹ. Nhưng, tôi chỉ muốn đặt một câu hỏi rằng : 
Nội chiến thì sao lại có quân đồng minh? Nếu Mỹ chỉ có tài trợ cho Việt Nam Cộng Hoà thì tôi đồng ý đây là nội chiến. Nhưng mà đã trực tiếp tham chiến và bức hại người Việt Nam thì là xâm lược nhé!
Các con giời bớt bợ dá* bố Mẽo đi.

Số liệu thì ở bên dưới đây:

1. Hoa Kỳ (Mỹ):

Khoảng 2,7 triệu lính Mỹ từng phục vụ tại Việt Nam.

Đỉnh điểm vào năm 1969, có khoảng 543.000 lính Mỹ đóng quân tại đây.


2. Hàn Quốc:

Gửi tổng cộng hơn 300.000 binh sĩ đến Việt Nam từ năm 1964 đến 1973.

Đỉnh điểm khoảng 50.000 lính cùng lúc.


3. Úc (Australia):

Gửi khoảng 60.000 binh sĩ tham chiến từ năm 1962 đến 1972.


4. New Zealand:

Có khoảng 3.500 binh sĩ được điều sang Việt Nam.


5. Philippines:

Gửi một lực lượng phi chiến đấu khoảng 10.000 người, chủ yếu là nhân viên y tế và công binh.


6. Thái Lan:

Có khoảng 11.000 lính Thái được điều đến Việt Nam trong giai đoạn 1965–1972.


Tổng cộng, hơn 3 triệu lính đồng minh (chủ yếu là Mỹ) đã từng tham gia chiến tranh Việt Nam, trong đó phần lớn là từ Hoa Kỳ và Hàn Quốc.

The French Indochina War, also known as the First Indochina War, was a conflict fought between France and the Viet Minh (Democratic Republic of Vietnam) from 1946 to 1954. The Viet Minh, led by Ho Chi Minh, aimed to gain independence from French colonial rule in Vietnam. The war was characterized by a prolonged struggle, with the Viet Minh ultimately defeating the French at the Battle of Dien Bien Phu in 1954, leading to French withdrawal from Indochina. 
Key Events and Outcomes:
• French Colonialism:
After World War II, France sought to reassert its colonial control over Indochina (including Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia). 
• Viet Minh Rise:
The Viet Minh, initially formed to resist the Japanese occupation during the war, grew in strength and began fighting against French forces in 1946. 
• Prolonged Conflict:
The war was characterized by a stalemate for several years, with the French possessing a military advantage but facing logistical challenges and rising public opposition to the war in France. 
• Battle of Dien Bien Phu:
This decisive battle in 1954 resulted in a major French defeat, as the Viet Minh effectively neutralized the French garrison. 
• French Withdrawal:
The French defeat at Dien Bien Phu led to the Geneva Accords in 1954, which officially ended the war and resulted in the French withdrawal from Indochina. 
• Indochina Division:
The Geneva Accords also divided Vietnam into North and South, with the communist Viet Minh controlling the North.

comment_text:
Hi Netflix, as a Vietnamese, I find the title “The Vietnam War” misleading. This was not “Vietnam’s war”, it was a war of American intervention in a sovereign country. In Vietnam, we call it “The Resistance War Against The U.S Imperialists.”
Names matter. When history is told mainly from the viewpoint of the one who invaded, it risks silencing those who suffered the most.
If this series wants to highlight overlooked perspectives, it should start by respecting the truth and that begins with the name.

Là một người Việt Nam, tôi thấy tên "Chiến tranh Việt Nam" gây hiểu nhầm. Đây không phải là Chiến tranh Việt Nam, mà là cuộc xâm lược của Mỹ vào một đất nước có chủ quyền. Ở Việt Nam chúng tôi gọi đây là "Cuộc kháng chiến chống đế quốc Mỹ".
Cách gọi rất quan trọng. Khi lịch sử được kể từ góc nhìn của người đi gây chiến, thì câu chuyện của những người bị xâm lược rất dễ bị lãng quên. Nếu bộ phim thật sự muốn tôn trọng những góc nhìn bị bỏ qua, thì nên bắt đầu bằng sự thật – và sự thật bắt đầu từ cách gọi tên cho đúng.

video_title: Turning Point: The Vietnam War | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: The accurate name of the war is not the "Vietnam War" but the **Resistance War Against American Imperialist Aggression**. The U.S. government sought to invade Vietnam, violating the Geneva Accords by replacing French colonialists and intervening in Southern Vietnam to plot the division of our nation. Over 3 million U.S. troops were deployed during the conflict, peaking at nearly 638,000 soldiers on Vietnamese battlefields. The U.S. dropped 15.38 million tons of bombs on Vietnam—3.8 times the amount used in World War II—leaving lasting consequences, including victims of Agent Orange poisoning, that persist to this day. The U.S. invasion plot and wartime atrocities are undeniable, and no distorted narratives, regardless of perspective, can whitewash this truth. On April 30, 2025, the Socialist Republic of Vietnam will commemorate the 50th anniversary of liberating the South and reunifying the country, connecting its mountains and rivers as one. Therefore, it is essential to correctly understand the nature of this war under its rightful name: **the Resistance War Against American Imperialist Aggression**.

video_title: Turning Point: The Vietnam War | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
In essence, the Vietnamese name, "The Resistance War Against The U.S. Imperialists," provides a more accurate reflection of the conflict from their viewpoint. It highlights the external aggression they faced and their determined resistance to achieve national liberation. Using the Western term can, from their perspective, obscure these crucial aspects of their history.

From the Vietnamese perspective, calling it "The Vietnam War" has several limitations and can be seen as inaccurate or even disrespectful:

It centers the conflict around Vietnam, potentially obscuring the primary role of external intervention. The name doesn't explicitly acknowledge that the war was largely initiated and driven by foreign powers, particularly the United States. "The Resistance War Against the U.S. Imperialists" clearly identifies the main external actor and the nature of the conflict as one of resistance against that intervention.

It can downplay the agency and the objectives of the Vietnamese people. Calling it simply "The Vietnam War" might inadvertently suggest an internal conflict or a war solely contained within Vietnam's borders. The Vietnamese name emphasizes their active role in resisting foreign domination and fighting for national liberation and unification.

It doesn't fully capture the Vietnamese experience of the war. For the Vietnamese, this wasn't just "a war" that happened in their country. It was a struggle for their very existence, their independence, and their right to self-determination against a powerful foreign entity. The name they use reflects the immense sacrifices and the nature of their fight.

"Vietnam's war" could be misconstrued to imply responsibility for the conflict. The Vietnamese perspective is that they were defending their sovereignty against external aggression. The term "The Vietnam War" might unintentionally shift some of the focus of responsibility onto Vietnam itself, rather than the intervening powers.

comment_text:
The Domino Theory was a big reason the U.S. got involved in Vietnam. Here’s how it played out:

⸻

The Idea:

The U.S. believed that if one country in Southeast Asia fell to communism, the others nearby would fall too — like dominoes.
	•	First Vietnam…
	•	Then maybe Laos, Cambodia…
	•	Then Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia…

And that could threaten U.S. allies and interests in the region.

⸻

The Goal:

Western countries (especially the U.S.) wanted to “contain” communism — stop it from spreading beyond where it already existed.

So when North Vietnam (communist) started trying to take over South Vietnam (non-communist), the U.S. stepped in to support the South and prevent another “domino” from falling.

⸻

In Reality:
	•	The Domino Theory had some truth — Cambodia and Laos did become communist not long after South Vietnam fell.
	•	But it didn’t spread all over Asia like feared.
	•	The Vietnam War ended up being super costly and controversial for the U.S.
1. Vietnam Falls (1975)
	•	After years of war, North Vietnam defeated South Vietnam.
	•	Saigon (the South’s capital) fell in April 1975, and the country was reunified under communist rule.

⸻

2. Cambodia Falls Too (1975)
	•	Just days before Saigon fell, the Khmer Rouge (a radical communist group) took over Cambodia.
	•	Led by Pol Pot, they wanted to create a pure, agrarian communist society.

⸻

3. The Killing Fields Begin (1975–1979)
	•	Pol Pot began a brutal genocide:
	•	Forced city people into the countryside for labor
	•	Killed anyone seen as “educated” or “Western-influenced” — doctors, teachers, people with glasses even
	•	Nearly 2 million people died from execution, starvation, or forced labor

⸻

4. Vietnam Eventually Intervened (1979)
	•	Vietnam (ironically, now a communist country too) invaded Cambodia and removed Pol Pot from power.
	•	The Khmer Rouge fled, but the scars of the genocide lasted decades.

⸻

So yes — the fall of Vietnam to communism set off a chain reaction that helped lead to:
	•	Communism in Cambodia and Laos
	•	One of the worst genocides in the 20th century

comment_text: I'd like to recommend a trilogy of books to anyone who really wants to dig deep into Vietnam. 😊There is a trilogy of books on the Vietnam war from the historian Mark Moyar. Two of the three books in the trilogy have been published - Triumph Forsaken: The Vietnam War, 1954-1965 and Triumph Regained: The Vietnam War, 1965-1968. The third book is forthcoming. One doesn't need to agree with Moyar to benefit from his work (I don't entirely agree with him), and indeed Moyar's books have started a firestorm of debate among historians and other scholars of the war, with lots of debate in print and in person. Regardless of one's thoughts and opinions about Vietnam, Moyar's work is engaging and well worth wrestling with.

video_title: Turning Point: The Vietnam War | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
While this documentary may seem like a tribute to Vietnam, using the term “Vietnam War”—especially right before April 30th—is deeply problematic and reveals a biased narrative.

This was not a “civil war” between North and South Vietnam. It was a war of resistance against foreign invasion. The United States directly intervened, dropped millions of tons of bombs, sprayed toxic chemicals like Agent Orange, and caused unimaginable suffering to the Vietnamese people. Calling it the “Vietnam War” erases the context of colonialism, nationalism, and the justified struggle for independence.

To release such a documentary during a sensitive time, without acknowledging the war as a foreign aggression, feels less like honoring Vietnam and more like monetizing its pain—especially when the storytelling remains rooted in a Western perspective.

If this film is truly meant to honor Vietnam’s history and people, then the title and framing must reflect that truth. Consider changing the English title to something more accurate and respectful—such as “Vietnam’s Resistance Against U.S. Aggression” or “The War for Vietnam’s Liberation”. Words matter. So does perspective.

video_title: Turning Point: The Vietnam War | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Series Turning Point của Netflix sắp ra mắt phần “Vietnam War” vào đúng dịp kỷ niệm 50 năm Ngày Giải Phóng Miền Nam 30/4 của đất nước ta - cái kết đẹp cho cuộc chiến chống lại sự xâm lược của Mỹ cùng với sự bành trướng, sự chia rẽ, sự bán nước của những kẻ phản bội tổ quốc. Nó được trông đợi bởi giới phê bình rằng đánh dấu một bước ngoặt trong cách nhìn nhận lịch sử đầy cảm xúc và phức tạp. Tuy vậy trong phần trước, “The Bomb and The Cold War”, nhiều chi tiết lịch sử đã được trình bày với một góc nhìn khá hẹp hòi và thiếu đi sự đa chiều, khiến người xem như bị hạn chế trong một “vùng an toàn” của những quan điểm quen thuộc.

Cảm giác của tôi khi theo dõi phần trước là như thể bị che giấu nửa sự thật – khi mà những câu chuyện lịch sử vĩ đại lại bị cắt cụt và thu hẹp thành những mảnh ghép không trọn vẹn. Người xem chỉ được nghe một hướng, mà bỏ qua những khía cạnh phức tạp như các sai sót trong hành động của các liên minh quốc tế, hay những nỗ lực đối thoại hết sức thiết yếu từ phía Nga. Việc giảm nhẹ hay đưa qua loa những nguyên nhân cốt lõi của các cuộc xung đột, chẳng hạn như cuộc khủng hoảng tên lửa Cuba, thật sự để lại một khoảng trống không nhỏ trong tâm trí khán giả.

Tôi hoàn toàn cảm thông với những nỗi đau do chiến tranh để lại, đặc biệt là với người dân Ukraine – những người không hề mong muốn trở thành nạn nhân trong cuộc đối đầu quyền lực. Tuy nhiên, bản chất của vấn đề không chỉ đơn giản là đứng về phía một bên, mà là trách nhiệm phải kể lại lịch sử một cách trung thực và trọn vẹn nhất có thể. Phim tài liệu này, dù có thể hiểu được là một phần trong cuộc chiến tranh thông tin, nhưng cũng cần phải đặt ra câu hỏi lớn: Liệu đây có thực sự là nỗ lực đưa ra một cái nhìn đa chiều, hay chỉ còn là một mảnh ghép thấm đượm định kiến?

Không có tác phẩm nào có thể hoàn toàn trung thực và không thiên vị khi kể về những sự kiện lịch sử phức tạp như thế. Điều quan trọng là khán giả nên luôn biết cách lắng nghe, tìm tòi thêm các nguồn thông tin và tự rút ra nhận định của riêng mình thông qua tư duy phản biện. Có lẽ, phần “Vietnam War” sắp tới của Turning Point sẽ là cơ hội để chúng ta chứng kiến một nỗ lực hết sức chân thành trong việc chạm tới những cảm xúc sâu sắc của lịch sử, nhưng cũng có lẽ nó sẽ lại là một chiến dịch tuyên truyền tẩy trắng lịch sử, phủi sạch công lao những cá nhân đã góp phần định hình nên lịch sử.

Hi Netflix, as a Vietnamese, I find the title “The Vietnam War” misleading. This was not “Vietnam’s war”, it was a war of American intervention in a sovereign country. In Vietnam, we call it “The Resistance War Against The U.S Imperialists.”
Names matter. When history is told mainly from the viewpoint of the one who invaded, it risks silencing those who suffered the most.
If this series wants to highlight overlooked perspectives, it should start by respecting the truth and that begins with the name.

comment_text:
I've got goosebumps every time someone mentions the story of how the Americans tried to invade our fellow Vietnamese people during the period from 1954 to 1975. I am very proud that my country is now unified and enjoys peace, but I also feel deep sorrow when I see the painful images of my fellow Vietnamese people, as shown in historical footage.

If someone asks me how to truly feel the pain and loss of that time, I would say: visit the War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City at least once. We should all understand the cost of war in order to better appreciate what we have when living in peace.

On the 50th anniversary of the liberation of the South and the reunification of Vietnam, I would like to send my deepest gratitude to the heroes who bravely sacrificed themselves so that I could live the peaceful life I have today. I hope that Vietnam’s story will serve as an inspiration for the world to pursue and cherish peace. 

We Vietnamese people do not forget the painful losses caused by war, but we are always ready to welcome you to experience Vietnam with sincerity. Sending love to the world from the brave and resilient people of Vietnam.

comment_text:
As a Vietnamese heritage with an entire family line that fought for the People's Army of Vietnam, I can understand why the Vietnamese were able to win that war.

They were incredibly brave and willing to sacrifice everything for their ideals. During the Tet Offensive of 1968, there were entire units that fought to the last man. One story that stands out is that of Võ Văn Điều, a regimental commander during the campaign. His regiment fought all the way to the American headquarters, where most of them were killed in action. Severely wounded, Võ Văn Điều handed his pistol to a female comrade and told her, “Take this pistol, pretend to be a refugee, and make your way to the revolutionary base. Report to our superiors that the regiment completed its mission—fighting to the very last man.”

They had lived through and witnessed the brutality of colonial regimes and their collaborators, which forged in them an unbreakable will to resist. My grandfather’s younger brother was a local guerrilla fighter. During an ambush on American and ARVN troops near our home, he was discovered and killed. After the battle, U.S. and ARVN soldiers forced my entire family to watch as his body was tied to a Jeep and dragged from his house to a mass grave.

This is what my grandparents—who lived through and fought during that time told me.
